comment.body:
In my calculations, I’m seeing the 21D SPY/TNX Beta hitting the highest value in the last 5 years (100th percentile). Additionally, the 10D SPY/TLT correlation is at an extremely negative level. So, if the Fed today, as ALMA suggests, acknowledges the latest data (i.e., inflation is slowing down, the economy has slightly decelerated due to tariffs, etc.) and leans slightly dovish, then the market will, as ALMA puts it, recognize this as good news. A mildly dovish Fed would generally have a negative impact on yields and a positive effect on SPY and TLT, leading to a reversal of my extreme values (i.e., reversal of the extremely high 21D SPY/TNX Beta and reversal of the extremely negative 10D SPY/TLT correlation).
Please correct me if I’m misinterpreting anything. But this would also align with ALMA’s bias mentioned above. Plus, there are some key points in the Weekly Post worth noting:
"Meanwhile, certain circles on the Street are saying we will see positive signals from the FED this week."
"Spot/vol beta has been trending downward! This is a major hidden strength in the current momentum."
"Both I and my algo have noticed that most funds have switched to high-frequency mean reversion trading."

comment.body: To clear up some of the confusion: Quarterly Opex is the OPEX in the third week of March, June, Sep, Dec. Not the end of the quarter. It's important because the quarterly option contracts are the most liquid and usually have the highest open interest. That's also when the quarterly futures (ES, NQ, etc) expire

comment.body: if they pause QT so that they are not draining reserves until TGA is refilled. That will be a double whammy. Because at the moment the TGA is spending the nations savings account rather than issuing new bonds until the debt ceiling is raised. That means money that is siting dormant in the TGA usually is spent into the economy. It has the effect of holding rates down because there is a reduced supply of bonds. so economy and asset prices are juiced until they start to refill the TGA...when the opposite effect takes hold and rates rise more rapidly because Treasury is offering much higher amounts of bonds to refill TGA

comment.body: Today was my first day  using it, but basically set up the dashboard as shown with gamma heat map on the left, charm on the right, positioning ODTE) in the  middle.  I'd bought a lotto SPX5700 before FOMC at $3, as well as a few SPY566C ODTE, and as price started moving up, you can basically see the probable path with negative gamma above (dealer's buying as spot going up) and supportive charm (same) -- this screenshot is zoomed in a bit towards end of day. So it gave me confidence to hold the lotto a lot longer than I normally would  have, that plus Alma's levels. comment.body: My thinking (which may not be 100% correct and open to correction) was that once we saw SPX/ESM hold key levels and then the bid start coming in and remain supportive after the 2pm press release, like Alma predicted in her Weekly Post -- and we are in negative gamma [MMs buy with move up in spot] and negative charm [also MM buy with move up in spot], therefore price under the customer and MM buying pressure in this environment is going to be like water flowing towards the lowest point on a surface, here the most negative gamma area (the darker red, and yellow line which marks the bottom of the trough), and that's basically what happened.  It sort of slipped  under and past one suppressive charm area under very negative gamma (the last push to 5715 or so), then gamma became less negative (less MM support) as the bid dried up, price dropped, the next push ran into the suppressive charm (the yellow at 3:50pm or so) and green local gamma high (low absolute value, still negative, but "uphill" to the crest of a little ridge relative to the areas all around it).  Those two together resisted the move higher and price dropped again into supportive charm (blue) and another yellow gamma local low, at the close.

comment.body:
@RE 
You asked for this, so here goes 


 The YLD family of funds sells covered calls on a range of underlying indices, including the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), S&P 500, and Russell 2000. While that broad selling impacts trading into Friday’s expiration, as billions in notional delta get sold across major indices, today’s focus is on QYLD — which specifically targets the NDX.

QYLD systematically sells billions of dollars in at-the-money NDX calls for the following month’s AM expiration. These positions are typically bought back the Thursday before expiration — which is today — as part of their roll process.

According to their strategy, QYLD executes these buybacks throughout the day at the volume-weighted average price (VWAP). Although no specific time is disclosed, I’ve noticed that volume tends to pick up significantly between 1:00 and 2:00 PM ET during this window. That flow often impacts index movement, providing a supportive or bullish push as negative delta is bought back and positive delta is added into the market.
